main_summary.summary: Grace Hall Miller, born July 21, 1932 in Baker County, Georgia, was interviewed March 9, 2013 at Albany State University by Will Griffin for the Civil Rights History Project. The sixth and youngest child of farmer Joe Nathan Hall and homemaker Nanny Hall, Miller secretly married Hosie Miller Sr. in 1946 at age 14 at the probate court; they had five daughters and one son and farmed in Baker County for eighteen years. In 1958 Grace and Hosie Miller became the first two Black voters to register in Baker County; that same period saw bombings of the cars of Mr. Cunningham and Mr. Ackie King and the framing of Mr. Cunningham with planted stomp whiskey by Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson. In March 1965 Hosie Miller was shot in the back by a white relative in a dispute over a cow that had strayed into their pasture; he died after a week, leaving Grace pregnant with their son Hosie Jr., born that June. After her husband's killing the Miller home became a base for the Baker County movement; Charles Sherrod of SNCC came in and later married Miller's oldest daughter Shirley. Miller was elected to the Baker County Board of Education and served until 2013. She describes a cross-burning at her home, federal Justice Department intervention in voter registration, and the 2010 USDA controversy over Shirley Sherrod.
key_themes: widowhood and family resilience after a racially motivated killing
Black women in local elected office
main_summary.historical_significance:
Miller's testimony documents the Baker County, Georgia, freedom struggle from the perspective of a woman whose family was among the first to register and whose husband was killed by a white neighbor in 1965. The interview supplies first-person detail on the 1958 bombings of activists' cars and homes (Joshua Williams, Mr. Cunningham, Mr. Ackie King), the local enforcement role of Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson, Justice Department intervention against changed registration forms, and the use of the Miller home as a SNCC base where Charles Sherrod organized.

The interview also provides family-side context for two figures in the national record: Shirley Sherrod, Miller's oldest daughter and later USDA Georgia state director of rural development, whose 2010 firing Miller recounts from a mother's vantage; and the Spooner family rescue story that Shirley Sherrod referenced in her NAACP speech. Miller's narration links 1950s Baker County voter-registration violence, 1965 racial murder, late-1960s school integration, and twenty-first-century school-board politics within one continuous local biography.

chapters.title: Family and Upbringing in Baker County
chapters.summary: Miller introduces herself as Grace Hall Miller, born July 21, 1932, the youngest of six children of farmer Joe Nathan Hall and homemaker Nanny Hall. She describes her parents as church workers, her father as deacon of the church, and the moral instruction she received about honesty, not stealing, and treating others fairly.

chapters.title: Schooling and Teachers
chapters.summary: Miller describes walking two miles to a one-room rural school where students supplied their own firewood while white children rode the bus past throwing spitballs. She attended Newton Colored High School in Baker County and names teachers including Miss Granberry and Miss Smith from Albany and principal Bradley. She recalls being taught Bible verses, songs, poems, the Pledge of Allegiance, and home economics.

chapters.title: Secret Marriage at Fourteen
chapters.summary: Miller recounts meeting Hosie Miller Sr. at church Bible Training Union, secretly marrying him at age 14 in 1946 at the probate court when no blood test was required, and his brother later informing her parents. She describes her parents' shock, learning to cook and keep house as a young bride, and the eighteen-year marriage that followed.
related_events: Marriage of Grace Hall and Hosie Miller Sr., 1946

chapters.title: Husband's Murder in 1965
chapters.summary: Miller describes the March 1965 killing of her husband Hosie Miller Sr., shot in the back by George Williamson, a white relative, during a dispute over a cow that had been pastured at the Miller farm for three months. Hosie lingered about a week and died at the Albany hospital. Miller was pregnant with their son Hosie Jr., born that June; her husband had been certain the baby would be a boy and had painted the nursery blue.
related_events: Killing of Hosie Miller Sr., Baker County, Georgia, March 15, 1965

chapters.title: Failed Prosecutions and Family Ties
chapters.summary: Miller describes three failed attempts to bring her husband's killer to trial: an all-white Baker County jury that did not indict, a venue change to Albany, and a federal proceeding. The shooter was never convicted. Miller notes he was a relative of her father and a man she had grown up around; she avoided him until his death.
related_events: Three failed prosecutions of George Williamson, 1965

chapters.title: First Voter Registration in Baker County, 1958
chapters.summary: Miller and her husband became the first two Black voters to register in Baker County in 1958, accompanied by her sister Josephine Miller, who registered the same day. Miller describes the registrar's hands shaking too badly to write. They organized neighbors to register; county officials then replaced the small citizenship card with a long-form sheet, prompting Justice Department intervention that reverted the form.
related_events: First Black voter registration in Baker County, 1958

chapters.title: Bombings and Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson
chapters.summary: Miller describes 1958 retaliations against Baker County Black registrants: the bombing of her uncle Joshua Williams's house, the bombing of Mr. Ackie King's car and the car of Mr. Cunningham (also called 'card brought away' in the transcript), and the framing of Mr. Cunningham with planted stomp whiskey, leading to his two-week jailing until a Bainbridge, Georgia lawyer bonded him out. She names Sheriff 'Gator' Johnson as the local enforcer.
related_events: 1958 retaliatory bombings against Baker County Black registrants

chapters.title: Charles Sherrod, SNCC, and Home as Movement Base
chapters.summary: After her husband's 1965 killing, Miller's home in Baker County became a base for the Baker County movement. Charles Sherrod of SNCC came in, later married Miller's oldest daughter Shirley, and SNCC stayed in Baker County about two years. The local FmHA representative tried to seize her house for hosting movement workers; Miller called the Justice Department in Washington, which confirmed her mortgage was current and warned the agent off.
related_events: Marriage of Charles Sherrod and Shirley Miller

chapters.title: Cross-Burning and School Integration
chapters.summary: Miller describes the cross-burning at her home during the school-integration period, when her daughters were among the first Black students at the integrated school. Armed with her late husband's pump gun, she fired at the men in her yard from her porch. A bullet later broke her window at night. She also describes a daughter being summoned to the white superintendent's office on a fabricated complaint about liking a white boy.

chapters.title: Running for the School Board
chapters.summary: Miller describes paying her thirty-five-dollar filing fee, learning she lived in a different district than expected but choosing to run anyway, defeating two white candidates in a runoff, and serving on the Baker County Board of Education until 2013. She campaigned door-to-door, helping farm families shell peas and pick butter beans while talking with voters. She names Mr. Roy Kelly, an elderly white voter who praised her father Joe Nathan Hall for once returning his lost billfold.

chapters.title: Education Today and the New Board Conflict
chapters.summary: Miller describes a recent conflict in which an all-white voting bloc on the Baker County board moved to oust the Black superintendent who had taken the district from debt, against her judgment and during the CRCT testing window. She describes a community meeting on February 28 with about 200 attendees opposing the action in a 90% Black school system.
related_events: February 28 community meeting against superintendent removal, c. 2013

chapters.title: Shirley Sherrod and the Spooner Family
chapters.summary: Miller recounts the 2010 USDA controversy in which her daughter Shirley Sherrod was forced to pull over on the highway and resign her position via BlackBerry over a selectively edited NAACP speech. Miller describes calling Shirley after hearing the news, and recounts the underlying Spooner family story: Shirley's earlier work to save a white farm family from foreclosure, the Spooners' call to CNN to defend her, and their continued friendship including an invitation to a ninetieth birthday party.

chapters.title: Legacy and Closing Reflections
chapters.summary: Miller reflects on turning eighty, having a school road and a community road named for her during her lifetime, and the importance of teaching younger generations the history of segregation. She notes that younger people often do not know about segregated water fountains and waiting rooms, and worries about generational gaps in historical knowledge.
related_events: Naming of Grace Miller Road and school road in Baker County
